Next week, if the markets agree, Bill Ackman will probably be successful in=
 standing up the largest ever closed-end equity fund with an I.P.O. expecte=
d to raise as much as $10 billion. He=E2=80=99s calling it Pershing Square =
USA, and it would actually be the largest closed-end fund by a factor of at=
 least two, and the first issuance of a closed-end fund since 2022.

Pershing Square USA is yet another way for Ackman to accumulate more perman=
ent capital, as he likes to say=E2=80=94emulating his hero Warren Buffett=
=E2=80=99s Berkshire Hathaway model, where other peoples=E2=80=99 money is =
invested and reinvested at will with an unlimited time horizon. At the mome=
nt, Ackman has $19 billion under management, of which he reports in public =
filings that 88 percent is =E2=80=9Cpermanent=E2=80=9D capital=E2=80=94mean=
ing he=E2=80=99s got the money and can keep investing
it. Any equity holder who wants their money back can just sell their shares=
 in his existing publicly traded vehicle, Pershing Square Holdings, which t=
rades on the Euronext exchange. Pershing Square USA will add a maximum of a=
nother $10 billion (despite leaks suggesting it could be as much as $25 bil=
lion; I suspect the savvy Ackman will cut things off at less than $10 billi=
on to create perceived scarcity) to Ackman=E2=80=99s capital stack and will=
 pretty much become the U.S. version of Pershing Square Holdings, and it wi=
ll trade on the NYSE.

Most of the investors in Pershing Square USA will likely end up being insti=
tutions, but it will also be a way for retail investors=E2=80=94anyone with=
 more than $50 to invest=E2=80=94to get in on the Ackman action. (His main =
hedge fund, Pershing Square L.P., is only for =E2=80=9Caccredited=E2=80=9D =
investors, meaning those with a net worth of at least $1 million, excluding=
 a primary residence, among other restrictions.) Pershing Square USA will i=
nvest in a
very similar portfolio as Bill=E2=80=99s hedge fund=E2=80=94he famously has=
 significant positions in a handful of companies, such as Chipotle, Howard =
Hughes Corp., and Restaurant Brands=E2=80=94plus his various hedging strate=
gies that he comes up with on occasion and, he says, account for some 25 pe=
rcent of his profits over the last 20 years. Essentially, Pershing Square U=
SA could be a way for retail investors to have a decent hedge against the i=
nevitable future market corrections, assuming Ackman is smart enough to fig=
ure out how and when to put on his big shorts, as he has done in the past. =
(He does make a point of saying he will no longer short individual stocks, =
as he did to his detriment once upon a time with Herbalife.)

Not surprisingly, on the investor roadshow for Pershing Square USA, Bill is=
 touting his immense success as an investor=E2=80=94his 22x return profile =
since 2004 (though the S&P outperformed his fund in various years)=E2=80=94=
and the lower fee structure on the new fund than
he usually charges. (=E2=80=9CNo performance fees,=E2=80=9D he boasts, alth=
ough the 2 percent fee is double the 1 percent fee most closed-end funds ch=
arge.) Pershing Square, his hedge fund, will invest $500 million in Pershin=
g Square USA and will not sell out of it for 10 years. The investment will =
consist of $450 million invested at the $50 I.P.O. price and $50 million of=
 preferred stock, with a 7.5 percent dividend. He=E2=80=99s also promising =
no corporate level or other =E2=80=9Centity-level=E2=80=9D taxation, which =
of course is something that even Buffett can=E2=80=99t offer as a corporate=
 entity. (Ackman came to the idea of the closed-end fund structure, apparen=
tly, after checking out a 900-page book on the topic from the Palo Alto pub=
lic library.)

Ackman is promising he will share lots of information with his new investor=
s, including quarterly investor conference calls=E2=80=94like every other p=
ublic company=E2=80=94and have an annual =E2=80=9Cinvestor day.=E2=80=9D He=
=E2=80=99s no doubt hoping that his shareholder meetings
will one day resemble Buffett=E2=80=99s powwows in Omaha. This effort at mo=
re transparency has already kicked off with an Ackman mea culpa: =E2=80=9CW=
e=E2=80=99ve made our share of significant mistakes over time,=E2=80=9D Bil=
l said in the video accompanying his online roadshow. But, he hastens to ad=
d, =E2=80=9CThe last six years, what we call the beginning of the =E2=80=98=
permanent capital=E2=80=99 era of Pershing Square, have been extraordinary.=

 He wrote that =E2=80=9Cthe president may be severely ill=E2=80=9D and that=
 =E2=80=9COccam=E2=80=99s razor=E2=80=9D is operative. He then cited =E2=80=
=9Cpolice sources=E2=80=9D to tweet that Biden =E2=80=9Csuffered an undiscl=
osed medical emergency during his time in Las Vegas.=E2=80=9D

Alas, his =E2=80=9Cmedical emergency=E2=80=9D was the fact that he had Covi=
d, which was later confirmed by his doctor, repeatedly, and released in var=
ious public memoranda. Bill Grueskin, the former Journal editor and Columbi=
a J-School professor, tweeted sarcastically that =E2=80=9CWatching Ackman r=
etweet his way into the fever swamps of insane conspiracy theories should g=
ive us all some joy this morning.=E2=80=9D

I=E2=80=99m not sure where any of this came from with Bill. He used to be a=
 quite rational and clear-thinking observer of
the political scene, even if he went down various rabbit holes, such as end=
orsing the presidential candidacy of Jamie Dimon and Robert F. Kennedy Jr. =
and Dean Phillips. But to be so obviously polarizing in the middle of tryin=
g to raise some $10 billion in new money is a fundraising strategy with whi=
ch I am not familiar.

Ackman: Myth & Legend

Bill Ackman is always up to something, usually years before other people ha=
ve figured out just what he=E2=80=99s doing, and years before they try to r=
eplicate it themselves. Sometimes, his ambition and his prescience have wor=
ked in his favor.

For instance, in 2012, Ackman created a SPAC, Justice Holdings, in the U.K.=
 of all places, and then arranged for a merger between the SPAC and Burger =
King, a portfolio company of 3G Capital, the Brazilian-American buyout firm=
, that valued Burger King at an enterprise value of $8.1 billion. The deal =
was incredibly successful, especially for 3G Capital, and helped pave the w=
ay for Burger King, now part of Restaurant Brands International, to become =
one of the most lucrative buyouts of all time, netting 3G a profit of some =
$19 billion on its initial equity investment of $1.6 billion. Ackman=E2=80=
=99s 23.35
million shares are worth around $1.7 billion these days.

And, of course, who can forget how, in three weeks at the beginning of the =
pandemic, Bill turned a $27 million investment in cheap credit default swap=
s into a fortune of $3.6 billion by betting first that credit yields would =
spike as the extent of the Covid pandemic became more widely known, and tha=
t the Federal Reserve would step in to save the credit markets through more=
 quantitative easing, sending the stock market to new heights. As I have wr=
itten before, to Bill=E2=80=99s credit, it was one of the greatest trades o=
f all time.

Sometimes things haven=E2=80=99t worked as well. When Bill tried the SPAC g=
ame again a decade later, right when it was all the rage, his Pershing Squa=
re Tontine Holdings flopped. Tontine Holdings had all sorts of clever bells=
 and whistles: It was the largest SPAC ever; he=E2=80=99d raised $4 billion=
 for the I.P.O.; he=E2=80=99d intentionally used a bunch of minority firms =
to underwrite the
offering; etcetera. But, in the end, he couldn=E2=80=99t get a deal done in=
 the two-year window and he liquidated the thing, returning the cash to inv=
estors, less the $35 million in underwriting fees, which Pershing Square ha=
d to absorb. And then there was his $4 billion loss on Valeant Pharmaceutic=
als or his roughly $1 billion loss shorting Herbalife or Bill=E2=80=99s cru=
sade against his alma mater Harvard, which he extended to MIT, and then to =
Business Insider when it wrote a story accusing his wife of plagiarism=E2=
=80=A6 Whatever happened to that lawsuit, anyway? (I=E2=80=99m told the two=
 sides are still trying to negotiate a settlement, which may or may not hap=
pen.)

His appetite for risk, as evidenced by these deals, may offer some insight =
into his freewheeling social media persona. But whether that alter ego is a=
n asset for Pershing Square USA remains to be seen, of course. In the Persh=
ing Square USA roadshow presentation video, Bill calls it =E2=80=9Cgood new=
s=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Ca benefit=E2=80=9D
to its investors that he will =E2=80=9Cbe completely unrestricted in terms =
of my ability to update shareholders about developments in the portfolio,=
=E2=80=9D and that he=E2=80=99ll use X to do it. =E2=80=9CWe intend to anno=
unce new investments, investment rationale, and address questions from shar=
eholders,=E2=80=9D he said. =E2=80=9CWe may, for example, talk about why we=
 put in place a hedge on interest rates or commodity prices or whatever Bla=
ck Swan event that we=E2=80=99re concerned about, and we look forward to ad=
dressing questions in a very transparent way.=E2=80=9D

=80=99m <b>William D.</b> <b>Cohan</b>. <br/><br/><b>Bill</b> <b>Ackman</b>=

reaming bid for the NBA=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9CC=E2=80=9D package. All of this =

nt private email, <u><a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI=

ger war.</li><br/><li><i>KKR=E2=80=99s wealth management play</i>: On Tuesd=

 nearly 200-year-old small brokerage and investment bank, with some 135 off=
ices across the United States. It=E2=80=99s not a strategic deal for KKR, t=
he =E2=80=9Calternative asset=E2=80=9D behemoth, with a market value of mor=
e than $100 billion. Rather, it=E2=80=99s just an equity investment from KK=
R=E2=80=99s 13th North American fund. KKR bought Janney from Penn Mutual Li=
fe
Insurance Company, in Philadelphia, which has owned the brokerage since 198=
2. Now, Janney will become a stand-alone, private company for the first tim=
e in 42 years, owned by KKR and presumably a number of the Janney executive=
s who will get equity in the deal. <br/><br/>This is probably more of a bet=

irst State. Still, it=E2=80=99s not often that alternative asset management=

ying to compete with Wall Street for underwriting and advisory assignments)=
=2E <br/><br/>Sometimes they work ou=

rren Buffett</b> got his head handed
to him after he bought into Salomon Brothers in the early =E2=80=9990s, jus=
t before a major bond scandal was revealed. On the other hand, his preferre=
d stock investments in Goldman Sachs, Bank of America, and GE after the 200=
8 financial crisis all seemed to have worked out just fine. As has Leucadia=
=E2=80=99s investment in Jefferies, the mid-sized investment bank. In fact,=
 Leucadia changed its name to Jefferies Financial Group, and its stock is t=
rading at or near its all-time high. This will be an interesting test case =
of the importance of aligned incentives on Wall Street and to see if employ=
ee ownership, along with KKR governance and investment, can unlock the anim=
al spirits at Janney for the first time in nearly a half-century.</li></ul>

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Next week, if the m=
arkets agree, <b>Bill Ackman</b> will probably be successful in standing up=
 the largest ever <i>closed-end</i> equity fund with an I.P.O. expected to =
raise as much as $10 billion. He=E2=80=99s calling it Pershing Square USA, =
and it would actually be the largest closed-end fund by a factor of at leas=
t two, and the first issuance of a closed-end fund since 2022.<br/><br/>Per=
shing Square USA is yet another way for Ackman to accumulate more <i>perman=
ent </i>capital, as he likes to say=E2=80=94emulating his hero <b>Warren Bu=
ffett</b>=E2=80=99s Berkshire Hathaway model, where other peoples=E2=80=99 =
money is invested and reinvested at will with an unlimited time horizon. At=
 the moment, Ackman has $19 billion under management, of which he reports i=
n public filings that 88 percent is =E2=80=9Cpermanent=E2=80=9D capital=E2=
=80=94meaning he=E2=80=99s got the money and can keep investing
it. Any equity holder who wants their money back can just sell their shares=
 in his existing publicly traded vehicle, Pershing Square Holdings, which t=
rades on the Euronext exchange. Pershing Square USA will add a maximum of a=
nother $10 billion (despite leaks suggesting it could be as much as $25 bil=
lion; I suspect the savvy Ackman will cut things off at less than $10 billi=
on to create perceived scarcity) to Ackman=E2=80=99s capital stack and will=
 pretty much become the U.S. version of Pershing Square Holdings, and it wi=
ll trade on the NYSE.<br/><br/>Most of the investors in Pershing Square USA=
 will likely end up being institutions, but it will also be a way for retai=
l investors=E2=80=94anyone with more than $50 to invest=E2=80=94to get in o=
n the Ackman action. (His main hedge fund, Pershing Square L.P., is only fo=
r =E2=80=9Caccredited=E2=80=9D investors, meaning those with a net worth of=
 at least $1 million, excluding a primary residence, among other restrictio=
ns.) Pershing Square USA will invest in a
very similar portfolio as Bill=E2=80=99s hedge fund=E2=80=94he famously has=
 significant positions in a handful of companies, such as Chipotle, Howard =
Hughes Corp., and Restaurant Brands=E2=80=94plus his various hedging strate=
gies that he comes up with on occasion and, he says, account for some 25 pe=
rcent of his profits over the last 20 years. Essentially, Pershing Square U=
SA could be a way for retail investors to have a decent hedge against the i=
nevitable future market corrections, assuming Ackman is smart enough to fig=
ure out how and when to put on his big shorts, as he has done in the past. =
(He does make a point of saying he will no longer short individual stocks, =
as he did to his detriment once upon a time with Herbalife.)<br/><br/>Not s=
urprisingly, on the investor roadshow for Pershing Square USA, Bill is tout=
ing his immense success as an investor=E2=80=94his 22x return profile since=
 2004 (though the S&P outperformed his fund in various years)=E2=80=94a=
nd the lower fee structure on the new fund than
he usually charges. (=E2=80=9CNo performance fees,=E2=80=9D he boasts, alth=
ough the 2 percent fee is double the 1 percent fee most closed-end funds ch=
arge.) Pershing Square, his hedge fund, will invest $500 million in Pershin=
g Square USA and will not sell out of it for 10 years. The investment will =
consist of $450 million invested at the $50 I.P.O. price and $50 million of=
 preferred stock, with a 7.5 percent dividend. He=E2=80=99s also promising =
no corporate level or other =E2=80=9Centity-level=E2=80=9D taxation, which =
of course is something that even Buffett can=E2=80=99t offer as a corporate=
 entity. (Ackman came to the idea of the closed-end fund structure, apparen=
tly, after checking out a 900-page book on the topic from the Palo Alto pub=
lic library.) <br/><br/>Ackman is promising he will share lots of informati=
on with his new investors, including quarterly investor conference calls=E2=
=80=94like every other public company=E2=80=94and have an annual =E2=80=9Ci=
nvestor day.=E2=80=9D He=E2=80=99s no doubt hoping that his shareholder mee=
tings
will one day resemble Buffett=E2=80=99s powwows in Omaha. This effort at mo=
re transparency has already kicked off with an Ackman mea culpa: =E2=80=9CW=
e=E2=80=99ve made our share of significant mistakes over time,=E2=80=9D Bil=
l said in the video accompanying his online roadshow. But, he hastens to ad=
d, =E2=80=9CThe last six years, what we call the beginning of the =E2=80=98=
permanent capital=E2=80=99 era of Pershing Square, have been extraordinary.=
=E2=80=9D<br/></td>

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">On the one hand, as=
 Ackman is arguing, Pershing Square USA may democratize the often byzantine=
 world of hedge fund management. But, like it or not, it also represents hi=
s effort to capitalize on his recently ascending culture-war celebrity. In =
his way, it seems, fanboy Ackman is taking a page from <b>Elon Musk</b>=E2=
=80=99s playbook as much as from Buffett=E2=80=99s. Many of you will find t=
hat kind of opportunism disturbing, even if it happens all the time in othe=
r spheres of American society. But Ackman clearly thinks investors will ben=
efit from the media=E2=80=99s infatuation with him, where he=E2=80=99s a qu=
asi-regular on CNBC and Bloomberg<i> et al</i>. (A spokesman for Pershing S=
quare declined to comment, but Ackman is not shy about promoting his newfou=
nd social media presence, hoping that perhaps a Musk-like cult following ma=
y emerge for him, too.)
<br/><br/>Fascinatingly, and revealingly, Ackman is explicitly pitching inv=
estors in his roadshow presentation that his presence on X, where he now ha=
s 1.3 million followers, is a virtue. This, alas, is a highly subjective in=
terpretation of Bill Ackman and his diatribes on X, where he=E2=80=99s beco=
me an increasingly polarizing presence ever since he made a modest $10 mill=
ion personal investment in Musk=E2=80=99s Twitter and where he seems to par=
rot many of Musk=E2=80=99s obsessions on the platform. Needless to say, few=
 other Wall Street types have taken to X with Ackman=E2=80=99s enthusiasm a=
nd alacrity. Many people in my world think that=E2=80=99s a mistake; Bill c=
learly disagrees. <br/><br/>There have been too many weird Bill Ackman post=
s on X lately to explicate them all=E2=80=94he=E2=80=99s a proud blue check=
er so he has unlimited space to go on stemwinders, and he uses it=E2=80=94b=
ut a few of his latest offerings will suffice to give one a sense of what h=
e=E2=80=99s been up to. After the assassination attempt on <b>Donald
Trump</b>, Ackman quickly took to X (as Musk did) to proclaim his fealty to=
 Trump and to =E2=80=9Cformally endorse=E2=80=9D him for president, somethi=
ng he=E2=80=99d been alluding to for months. Most hedge fund managers in th=
e middle of a roadshow would not alienate half the investing population by =
formally endorsing one candidate over another while also criticizing his op=
ponent. But Bill did. <br/><br/>He=E2=80=99s also going nuts with the consp=
iracy theories on X, where he has thrown his weight behind the notion that =
there may have been multiple shooters in the assassination attempt on Donal=
d Trump=E2=80=94he later <u><a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFp=
bF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJLZ0dRNXlKVkNWRUprOWhWSlE5Snltaz0iLCJocmVmIjo=
iaHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZGFpbHltYWlsLmNvLnVrL25ld3MvYXJ0aWNsZS0xMzY1NjE4MS9iaWxsLW=
Fja21hbi1kZWxldGVkLXR3ZWV0LWRvbmFsZC10cnVtcC1hc3Nhc3NpbmF0aW9uLXRoZW9yeS5od=
G1sP3V0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1EcnkrUG93ZGVyKy0rU1VCU0NSSUJFUlMrJTI4NyUyRjI0JTJGMjQl=
MjlcdTAwMjZ1dG1fY29udGVudD1EcnkrUG93ZGVyKy0rU1VCU0NSSUJFUlMrJTI4NyUyRjI0JTJ=
GMjQlMjlcdTAwMjZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsX2FjdGlvblx1MDAyNnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9Y3VzdG=
9tZXIuaW9cdTAwMjZ1dG1fdGVybT1mNmM2MDYxN2RhNDJhYmQxMmEiLCJpbnRlcm5hbCI6ImY2Y=
zYwNjE3ZGE0MmFiZDEyYSIsImxpbmtfaWQiOjk3NDA2M30/46a7468908f76d70ab2192e37cea=
7eae4c9640948296812bef1111a28889966e" style=3D"color:#d40000;text-decoratio=
n:none;font-weight:bold" class=3D"sdf">deleted the
tweet</a></u> that suggested the shooting may have been an inside job=E2=80=
=94and wondered if Biden actually signed the letter last weekend in which h=
e announced he was no longer running for a second term as president. He wro=
te that =E2=80=9Cthe president may be severely ill=E2=80=9D and that =E2=80=
=9COccam=E2=80=99s razor=E2=80=9D is operative. He then cited =E2=80=9Cpoli=
ce sources=E2=80=9D to tweet that Biden =E2=80=9Csuffered an undisclosed me=
dical emergency during his time in Las Vegas.=E2=80=9D <br/><br/>Alas, his =
=E2=80=9Cmedical emergency=E2=80=9D was the fact that he had Covid, which w=
as later confirmed by his doctor, repeatedly, and released in various publi=
c memoranda. <b>Bill Grueskin</b>, the former <i>Journal </i>editor and Col=
umbia J-School professor, tweeted sarcastically that =E2=80=9CWatching Ackm=
an retweet his way into the fever swamps of insane conspiracy theories shou=
ld give us all some joy this morning.=E2=80=9D <br/><br/>I=E2=80=99m not su=
re where any of this came from with Bill. He used to be a quite rational an=
d clear-thinking observer of
the political scene, even if he went down various rabbit holes, such as end=
orsing the presidential candidacy of <b>Jamie Dimon </b>and <b>Robert F. Ke=
nnedy Jr.</b> and <b>Dean Phillips</b>. But to be so obviously polarizing i=
n the middle of trying to raise some $10 billion in new money is a fundrais=
ing strategy with which I am not familiar. <br/></td>

; padding: 24px 0;"><b>Ackman: Myth & Legend</b></td>

         <tr class=3D"replica" name=3D"s8">
          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Bill Ackman is alwa=
ys up to something, usually years before other people have figured out just=
 what he=E2=80=99s doing, and years before they try to replicate it themsel=
ves. Sometimes, his ambition and his prescience have worked in his favor. <=
br/><br/>For instance, in 2012, Ackman created a SPAC, Justice Holdings, in=
 the U.K. of all places, and then arranged for a merger between the SPAC an=
d Burger King, a portfolio company of 3G Capital, the Brazilian-American bu=
yout firm, that valued Burger King at an enterprise value of $8.1 billion. =
The deal was incredibly successful, especially for 3G Capital, and helped p=
ave the way for Burger King, now part of Restaurant Brands International, t=
o become one of the most lucrative buyouts of all time, netting 3G a profit=
 of some <i>$19 billion</i> on its initial equity investment of $1.6 billio=
n. Ackman=E2=80=99s 23.35
million shares are worth around $1.7 billion these days. <br/><br/>And, of =
course, who can forget how, in three weeks at the beginning of the pandemic=
, Bill turned a $27 million investment in cheap credit default swaps into a=
 fortune of $3.6 billion by betting first that credit yields would spike as=
 the extent of the Covid pandemic became more widely known, and that the Fe=
deral Reserve would step in to save the credit markets through <i>more</i> =
quantitative easing, sending the stock market to new heights. As I have wri=
tten before, to Bill=E2=80=99s credit, it was one of the greatest trades of=
 all time. <br/><br/>Sometimes things haven=E2=80=99t worked as well. When =
Bill tried the SPAC game again a decade later, right when it was all the ra=
ge, his Pershing Square Tontine Holdings flopped. Tontine Holdings had all =
sorts of clever bells and whistles: It was the largest SPAC ever; he=E2=80=
=99d raised $4 billion for the I.P.O.; he=E2=80=99d intentionally used a bu=
nch of minority firms to underwrite the
offering; etcetera. But, in the end, he couldn=E2=80=99t get a deal done in=
 the two-year window and he liquidated the thing, returning the cash to inv=
estors, less the $35 million in underwriting fees, which Pershing Square ha=
d to absorb. And then there was his $4 billion loss on Valeant Pharmaceutic=
als or his roughly $1 billion loss shorting Herbalife or Bill=E2=80=99s cru=
sade against his alma mater Harvard, which he extended to MIT, and then to =
Business Insider when it wrote a story accusing his wife of plagiarism<i>=
=E2=80=A6 </i>Whatever happened to that lawsuit, anyway? (I=E2=80=99m told =
the two sides are still trying to negotiate a settlement, which may or may =
not happen.)<br/><br/>His appetite for risk, as evidenced by these deals, m=
ay offer some insight into his freewheeling social media persona. But wheth=
er that alter ego is an asset for Pershing Square USA remains to be seen, o=
f course. In the Pershing Square USA roadshow presentation video, Bill call=
s it =E2=80=9Cgood news=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Ca benefit=E2=80=9D
to its investors that he will =E2=80=9Cbe completely unrestricted in terms =
of my ability to update shareholders about developments in the portfolio,=
=E2=80=9D and that he=E2=80=99ll use X to do it. =E2=80=9CWe intend to anno=
unce new investments, investment rationale, and address questions from shar=
eholders,=E2=80=9D he said. =E2=80=9CWe may, for example, talk about why we=
 put in place a hedge on interest rates or commodity prices or whatever Bla=
ck Swan event that we=E2=80=99re concerned about, and we look forward to ad=
dressing questions in a very transparent way.=E2=80=9D <br/></td>
